David's question cuts to the heart of what a king's covenant really means. He seeks out anyone remaining from Saul's house—not to eliminate a rival threat, but to honor a promise made to Jonathan. This is not the predictable move of an ancient Near Eastern ruler consolidating power. The initial irony is sharp: Saul had hunted David mercilessly, yet David now deliberately searches for Saul's heir to show him kindness. The covenant David made with Jonathan was not merely a friendship pact between two warriors—it was a binding oath that transcended the political realities of their time. Years have passed since Jonathan's death at Mount Gilboa, and David has become king over all Israel. He could have forgotten his promise entirely. Instead, he uses his position not to avenge himself but to fulfill a debt of honor. When Ziba reveals that Jonathan's son Mephibosheth exists but is lame in both feet—hidden away in Lo-debar, a place whose name means "without pasture"—David doesn't hesitate. He brings the crippled young man into his own presence.

The moment Mephibosheth encounters David is laden with fear and vulnerability. He falls facedown, fully aware that he is the grandson of the previous king and therefore potentially a threat to David's throne. His terror is utterly reasonable; throughout the ancient world, dynastic rivals were routinely executed. Yet David's first words are "Do not be afraid"—a phrase that echoes God's own reassurances throughout Scripture. David then makes a stunning declaration: he will restore all of Saul's land to Mephibosheth and grant him a permanent place at the royal table. This is not a minor gesture of charity. The land represents inheritance, livelihood, and dignity. The table represents inclusion, honor, and the security of belonging to the king's household. David is saying, in effect: you will be treated as family, not as a potential enemy to be eliminated. Mephibosheth's response—"What is your servant, that you should show regard for a dead dog like me?"—reveals the depth of his despair and his inability to comprehend such mercy. He sees himself as worthless, as beneath consideration, yet David has already decided his worth.

What makes this chapter's impact so profound is that it shows us covenant grace made visible. David does not show Mephibosheth kindness because Mephibosheth earned it or deserved it. He does it because Jonathan deserved it, and because David's word matters more than political calculation. Mephibosheth brings nothing to the table—he is lame, powerless, and from a defeated line. Yet he receives everything: land, security, honor, and place. When Ziba is commanded to work the ground so that Mephibosheth will have food, the reversal is complete. The grandson of the king who once held the throne now eats at another king's table, cared for, included, protected. The chapter ends with the simple statement that "Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem, because he always ate at the king's table, and he was lame in both feet." The lameness is mentioned last, almost as an afterthought—because with David's covenant commitment, it no longer defines his existence. He has been relocated from obscurity to the center of power, not because he changed, but because someone kept their promise. This is what covenant looks like when it actually means something.
